title: JavaScript Concepts
response:
JavaScript has 8 data types, split into two groups:

Primitive (immutable, stored by value):
• string — text: 'hello'
• number — integers and floats: 42, 3.14
• bigint — very large integers: 9007199254740991n
• boolean — true or false
• undefined — variable declared but not assigned
• null — intentional absence of a value
• symbol — unique, immutable identifier

Reference (stored by reference):
• object — includes arrays, functions, dates, maps, etc.

Use typeof to check a value's type at runtime.
code_example:
console.log(typeof 'hello');        // 'string'
console.log(typeof 42);             // 'number'
console.log(typeof true);           // 'boolean'
console.log(typeof undefined);      // 'undefined'
console.log(typeof null);           // 'object' ← JS quirk!
console.log(typeof Symbol());       // 'symbol'
console.log(typeof {});             // 'object'
console.log(typeof []);             // 'object' (arrays are objects)
console.log(typeof function(){}); // 'function'
